coredata.dc:title: Two birds with one stone: One-pot hydrothermal treatment of dragon fruit peel to simultaneously prepare efficient biochar adsorbent and fluorescent carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Jisuan
Zhou, Li
coredata.dc:description: The abundance of dragon fruit peel (DFP) has led to increased focus on its use for value-added purposes. This work presents a simple one-pot hydrothermal method to synthesize efficient biochar adsorbent and fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from DFP. The hydrothermal process yields a solid biochar adsorbent and a liquid CQDs solution. The resulting DFP-derived biochar boasts a specific surface area of 138.04 m2 g−1, a mean pore diameter of 3.68 nm, and a negatively charged surface, suggesting its efficacy in adsorbing cationic dyes, such as neutral red (NR) and methyl violet (MV), as demonstrated in this study. The obtained CQDs have sizes ranging from 3–6 nm, good water dispersibility, and bright and tunable fluorescence. Additionally, the CQDs display exceptional fluorescence quenching properties towards Fe3+, demonstrating their potential as a sensing material for Fe3+ with a remarkable limit of detection of 1.13 μM and a wide linear range of 0–285 μM. The CQDs also show low cytotoxicity and efficient cellular uptake capabilities, suggesting potential applications in cell imaging and Fe3+ detection within cells. Overall, this study presents a promising approach for utilizing DFP byproduct due to its simple synthesis process and the exceptional characteristics of the resulting adsorbents and CQDs.

SEM images (a,b), FTIR spectrum (c), N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (d), and pore size distribution (e) of DFP-derived biochar adsorbent. (f) Zeta potential values of DFP-derived biochar adsorbent at different pHs.

The corresponding absorption spectra of NR/TTZ (d) and MV/TTZ (f) mixtures before and after adsorption.

Effects of solution pH (T = 293 K) (a), ionic strength (T = 293 K, pH = 7) (b), and temperature (pH = 7) (c) on the adsorption of NR and MV by DFT-derived biochar. The initial concentration of NR and MV is 300 mg L−1. (d) Plots of lnQe/Ce against 1/T for the adsorption of NR and MV onto the DFT-derived biochar.

Effect of contact time on the adsorption of NR (a) and MV (b) by the DFP-derived biochar and the correlation of diverse kinetic models (C0 = 300 mg L−1, T = 293 K, pH = 7). (a) TEM image of CQDs. Raman (b), FTIR (c), XPS survey (d), XPS C1s (e), and XPS N1s (f) spectra of CQDs.

I0 and I are the emission intensity of CQDs in the absence and the presence of Fe3+, respectively. (c) Emission spectra of CQDs after mixing Fe3+ (100 μM) for diverse time. head.ce:title: Two birds with one stone: One-pot hydrothermal treatment of dragon fruit peel to simultaneously prepare efficient biochar adsorbent and fluorescent carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The abundance of dragon fruit peel (DFP) has led to increased focus on its use for value-added purposes. This work presents a simple one-pot hydrothermal method to synthesize efficient biochar adsorbent and fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from DFP. The hydrothermal process yields a solid biochar adsorbent and a liquid CQDs solution. The resulting DFP-derived biochar boasts a specific surface area of 138.04 m2 g−1, a mean pore diameter of 3.68 nm, and a negatively charged surface, suggesting its efficacy in adsorbing cationic dyes, such as neutral red (NR) and methyl violet (MV), as demonstrated in this study. The obtained CQDs have sizes ranging from 3–6 nm, good water dispersibility, and bright and tunable fluorescence. Additionally, the CQDs display exceptional fluorescence quenching properties towards Fe3+, demonstrating their potential as a sensing material for Fe3+ with a remarkable limit of detection of 1.13 μM and a wide linear range of 0–285 μM. The CQDs also show low cytotoxicity and efficient cellular uptake capabilities, suggesting potential applications in cell imaging and Fe3+ detection within cells. Overall, this study presents a promising approach for utilizing DFP byproduct due to its simple synthesis process and the exceptional characteristics of the resulting adsorbents and CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dragon fruit is a very popular fruit, with millions of tons of dragon fruit produced every year in tropical and subtropical regions. Despite its popularity, the important byproduct of dragon fruit-dragon fruit peel (DFP) is often discarded, meaning a great waste of potential biomass resources (Mallampati et al., 2015). DFP is primarily composed of pectin, dietary fibre, and water-soluble pigment, making it a subject of keen interest for researchers (Chen et al., 2022; Le, 2022; Luo et al., 2014). They are actively exploring various ways to utilize the DFP byproduct for the preparation of valuable materials (Ahmad et al., 2021; Cheok et al., 2016; Gandla et al., 2021) and the extraction of functional compounds (Madane et al., 2020; Muhammad et al., 2014). However, to date, research on value-added applications of DFP is still limited (Jawad et al., 2021; Pramitasari et al., 2022), and effective methods need to be explored to tap into the application potential of DFP.

The discharge of synthetic dyes into water environment poses a great challenge in wastewater management due to the complex aromatic structure, non-degradable properties, and potential toxic effects of these compounds. Annually, thousands of tons of various dyes are produced globally, with an estimated 10–15% being discharged into natural water bodies, leading to substantial water pollution (Ismail et al., 2019; Ruan et al., 2019). Due to the challenge of biologically degrading synthetic dyes, tackling wastewater contamination has become a critical issue. Among the various available methods, adsorption is an exemplary approach for efficiently removing organic dyes from industrial wastewater due to its ease of operation, cost-effectiveness, and wide-ranging applicability (Deng et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2022). Various agricultural byproducts, such as peanut hull (Han et al., 2009), rice husk (Zou et al., 2010), peach gum (Huang et al., 2018), date seeds (Ali et al., 2022), and agricultural solid wastes (Kadirvelu et al., 2003), have been explored as bioadsorbents. However, research exploring the utilization of DFP as an efficient dye adsorbent remains notably sparse.

Iron is a critical element that is essential for the proper functioning of the human body. Inadequate levels of Fe3+ can result in anemia, which hinders the body's ability to transport oxygen to tissues, impacting overall health and well-being (Huang et al., 2021; Petdum et al., 2020). Therefore, it is crucial to detect Fe3+ accurately and efficiently. The traditional approach for detecting Fe3+ can be cumbersome and prohibitively costly. Nevertheless, the integration of fluorescent detection using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has emerged as a highly promising technique for detecting Fe3+ (Gao et al., 2021; Preethi et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2022). This is owing to the significant advantages offered by CQDs, including ease of use, speedy detection rates, and exceptional sensitivity in identifying Fe3+. In recent years, there has been a growing interest in the development of cost-effective carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from natural biomass sources, such as corncob (Jagannathan et al., 2021), roasted chickpea (Başoğlu et al., 2020), Borassus flabellifer (Nagaraj et al., 2022), and watermelon juice (Lu et al., 2018), for the detection of Fe3+. While there have been promising findings in this area, there remains a lack of research exploring the potential for producing highly sensitive and selective CQDs for Fe3+ detection from biomass by-products. This presents both a challenge and an opportunity for further innovation and investigation in this field.

In this contribution, we present a facile and effective one-pot hydrothermal method for converting DFP into two valuable products: a solid biochar adsorbent capable of efficiently removing cationic dyes from wastewater, and a liquid product of highly water-dispersible CQDs with tunable fluorescence and low cytotoxicity. The biochar adsorbent showed exceptional adsorption capacity for cationic dyes such as neutral red (NR) and methyl violet (MV), highlighting its potential as a promising adsorbent for wastewater treatment. The CQDs were found to exhibit unique fluorescence quenching behavior towards Fe3+, making them a promising fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection. We further investigated the utilization of these CQDs for cell imaging and Fe3+ detection within cells. This study not only presents a straightforward approach for producing functional materials from inexpensive and abundant DFP, but also suggests promising avenues for the utilization of other types of agricultural byproducts.

The chemicals used in the experiments, namely methyl violet (MV), neutral red (NR), tartrazine (TTZ), methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB), FeCl3•6 H2O, FeSO4•7 H2O, CuCl2•2 H2O, Pb(CH3COO)2•3 H2O, Ni(NO3)2•6 H2O, ZnNO3•2 H2O, CaCl2, Co(NO3)2•6 H2O, KCl, NaCl, MnCl2•4 H2O, CdCl2, and MgSO4 were purchased from Beijing Innochem Technology Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China) and used without further processing. The dragon fruit used in the experiment was bought from a supermarket in Guilin, China. Deionized water was utilized in all experimental procedures. The detailed molecular structure information of used dyes is shown in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of biochar adsorbent and fluorescent CQDs from DFP
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure ( Fig. 1), 30 g of fresh DFP was mixed with 30 mL of water and subjected to high-speed grinding to obtain juice before hydrothermal treatment. The resulting DFP juice was then combined with 5 mL of ethanol and transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The mixture was heated at 180 °C for 5 h, after which it was allowed to cool naturally and then subjected to centrifugation to separate the solid and liquid products. The obtained solids were washed with water and freeze-dried to obtain the final biochar adsorbent. The collected supernatant was subsequently dialyzed against water and concentrated using rotary evaporation treatment to obtain a solution of DFP-derived CQDs at a concentration of 0.2 mg/mL. Furthermore, the CQDs can be obtained in dried form for further characterization by undergoing a freeze-drying treatment. It is worth noting that the addition of an appropriate amount of ethanol can enhance the hydrothermal reaction, leading to the increased production of high-quality DFP-derived biochar and CQDs (see Fig. S1). In addition, the hydrothermal reaction was optimized by conducting it at a temperature of 180 °C for 5 h, as it led to the production of DFT-derived biochar and CQDs with improved adsorption and fluorescent properties compared to other reaction conditions (Table S2).

To assess the adsorption capabilities of the DFP-derived biochar adsorbent, a variety of cationic and anionic dyes were selected for experiments, including cationic MV, NR, and MB dyes and anionic TTZ, MO, and ART dyes. Calibration curves were created between absorbance and dye concentration for standard dye solutions at specific pH ranges (3−8) and temperatures (275 K, 293 K, and 313 K). Adjustments to solution pH were made using 0.1 mM HCl and NaOH solutions, while the influence of NaCl electrolyte was examined within the range of 0–1.0 M of NaCl. The impact of initial dye concentration (0.05–10 mM) on adsorption at pH 7 and 293 K was also evaluated, as well as contact time at the same conditions.

For adsorption experiments, 10 mg of freeze-dried DFP-derived biochar was combined with 10 mL of dye solution of known concentration and agitated at a specific temperature for a set amount of time. After adsorption, the resulting mixture was centrifuged, and supernatant absorption spectra were determined using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The adsorption capacity of the DFP-derived biochar adsorbent was calculated using the following equation (Tan et al., 2018; Watwe et al., 2023):

where Qe (mg g−1) is the amount of adsorption per gram of biochar adsorbent at adsorption equilibrium, C0 (mg L−1) is the initial dye concentration, Ce (mg L−1) is the equilibrium dye concentration, V (L) is the solution volume, and m (g) is the mass of the adsorbent used. For more comprehensive and detailed information regarding the models of adsorption thermodynamics, kinetics, and isotherms, readers can refer to the supplementary material.

To detect Fe3+, we utilized the DFP-derived CQDs as a fluorescent probe and monitored the fluorescence intensity for variations (Gao et al., 2021; Lu et al., 2018). A standard procedure was followed whereby a solution of CQDs (200 μL, 0.2 mg/mL) in Tris-HCl buffer (100.0 μL, 10 mM, pH 7.0) was added to multiple quartz vials. Subsequent to this, various concentrations of Fe3+ (or other metal ions ) in deionized water were added to the vials in order to make a final concentration of 0–285 μM. The total volume of the solutions was 3 mL, and fluorescence spectra for all solutions were recorded with excitation at 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of DFP-derived biochar for dye adsorption

ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the DFP-derived biochar adsorbent was examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The SEM image displays a distinctive layered structure with a rough surface texture ( Fig. 2a). Certain regions exhibits uneven topography while retaining the layered pattern (Fig. 2b), which can increase the specific surface area and enhance the number of adsorption sites, thereby making the biochar more effective for dye adsorption. The Raman spectrum shown in Fig. S2 exhibits two prominent carbon characteristic peaks at 1578 cm−1 and 1360 cm−1, which can be attributed to the G band and the D band, respectively. These peaks strongly indicate the successful formation of DFP-derived biochar. To obtain more information on the structure of the biochar adsorbent, FTIR and N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm tests were performed. Fig. 2c shows that the broad absorption peak at 3420 cm−1 corresponds to the characteristic absorption bands of –OH vibrations, whereas the two peaks detected at approximately 2940 cm−1 and 2855 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of C-H bonds. The peaks noted at 1635 cm−1 and 1060 cm−1 are linked to the vibrations of CO and C-O bonds, respectively. The FTIR spectrum thus confirmed the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the DFP-based adsorbent. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms curve indicates the presence of mesoporous structures in the DFP-derived biochar (Fig. 2d). A slight increase in adsorption is observed between a relative pressure (P/P0) ranges of 0–0.48, implying the existence of smaller pores (Huang et al., 2023; Soltani et al., 2020). However, a significant increase in adsorption is observed between a P/P0 range of 0.48–1.0, indicating the presence of larger pores and interparticle voids. The pore analysis curve displays two primary peaks centered at 2.3 and 3.9 nm (Fig. 2e), which confirms the presence of mesoporous structures in the biochar. Additionally, the specific surface area was measured to be as high as 138.04 m2 g−1. The combination of a large surface area and porous structure enhances the biochar's ability to transport dye molecules and thereby leads to superior adsorption performance. Because surface charges of the adsorbent can significantly affect dye adsorption (Eltaweil et al., 2020; Song et al., 2018), the zeta potential values of the adsorbent were determined (Fig. 2f). The results demonstrate that the biochar adsorbent possesses a negatively charged surface within the pH range of 3 to 9, which can be attributed to an abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups present on the biochar surface. This feature can significantly enhance the biochar's ability to capture cationic dyes, making it a promising adsorbent for dye removal applications.

To evaluate the adsorption performance of DFP-derived biochar, we tested its effectiveness against a range of dyes, including cationic NR, MV and MB dyes and anionic TTZ, ART and MO dyes. The biochar was mixed with dye solutions (5 mg L−1) for 1 h at pH 7. After this, we observed that the color of cationic dyes became significantly lighter, while the color of anionic dyes remained unchanged ( Fig. 3a). Additionally, we further determined the saturated adsorption capacities of the biochar for different dyes with high initial dye concentration (300 mg L−1). The results indicate that the adsorption capacities for cationic dyes were much higher than that of anionic dyes (Fig. 3b). For example, the adsorption capacities for NR and TTZ dyes were measured as 101.2 mg g−1 and 0.32 mg g−1, respectively. Based on these results, we anticipate that the DFP-derived biochar would possess excellent selective adsorption capability, making it highly suitable for practical dye separation. To confirm this hypothesis, we selected two dye mixtures, namely NR/TTZ and MV/TTZ, to assess the adsorption selectivity of the biochar (Fig. 3c-f). The dye mixture, after being mixed with the DFP-derived biochar, exhibited a very similar color to that of the anionic TTZ dye (Fig. 3c,e). This indicated that the biochar selectively captured the cationic dyes from the mixture.

Additionally, the adsorption process was monitored using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. Prior to adsorption, distinctive absorption peaks associated with the cationic dye (TTZ) and the anionic dyes (NR and MV) were observed (Fig. 3d,f). However, after adsorption for 20 min, the peak intensity of NR or MV in both NR/TTZ and MV/TTZ groups decreased significantly, while the peak corresponding to the TTZ did not exhibit notable change. Hence, we conclude that the DFP-derived biochar possesses high adsorption capacity and good adsorption selectivity towards cationic dyes. The exceptional adsorption capability of biochar towards cationic dyes can be attributed to its expansive surface area, porous structure, and abundant oxygen-containing functional groups which are associated with a negatively charged surface. To further evaluate the adsorption performance of DFP-derived biochar, we selected cationic NR and MV dyes for further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of solution pH, ionic strength and temperature on dye adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: The solution pH is crucial in determining the adsorption process due to its influence on both the surface charge state of the adsorbent and the molecular configuration and solubility of dyes (Gul et al., 2023; Ofomaja and Ho, 2007; Zhang et al., 2018; Zou et al., 2012). Fig. 4a illustrates that the adsorption capacity of DFP-derived biochar gradually increases with the incremental rise in pH. For instance, the adsorption amounts for NR dyes at pH 3 and pH 8 are 83.9 mg g−1 and 100.3 mg g−1, respectively. This trend can be attributed to the protonation of oxygen-containing groups in the adsorbent under alkaline conditions, which generates negatively charged adsorption sites. As the pH further increases, the density of negatively charged species on the surface of the biochar also increases, as evidenced by the zeta potential results (Fig. 2f), leading to stronger electrostatic attraction between the biochar and cationic dyes. In addition to the solution pH, various inorganic salts present in practical dye solutions can also impact the adsorption process (Li et al., 2018; Molavi et al., 2019). To investigate this, we chose NaCl as a representative inorganic salt. Typically, when electrostatic attraction governs adsorption, the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent decreases as ionic strength increases. We can observe a gradual decline in the adsorption capacity for both NR and MV with an increase in the NaCl concentration (Fig. 4b), indicating that the uptake of cationic dyes onto the biochar mostly relies on electrostatic attraction. Additionally, the conducted adsorption experiments at various temperatures revealed that the adsorption capacities of biochar for NR and MV exhibited a slight increase as the temperature increased (Fig. 4c). To enhance our comprehension of the adsorption behavior, we conducted a thorough investigation of the thermodynamic aspects by employing the van't Hoff equation (for further details, refer to supplementary material) (Duan et al., 2019; Rashidinia and Dinari, 2023). Fig. 4d depicts the plots of lnQe/Ce against 1/T, showing a good linear relationship, indicating that the adsorption process aligns well with the van't Hoff equation. The associated values for Gibbs free energy (∆G), enthalpy (ΔH), and entropy (∆S) were calculated and are presented in Table S3. The positive ΔH and ΔS values confirm that the uptake process of NR and MV dyes by DFP-derived biochar is an endothermic process and occurs randomly (Sharafzad et al., 2021). Additionally, the negative ∆G value suggests that the adsorption process is spontaneous and does not require external energy input.

The rate of adsorption is a crucial metric for assessing the effectiveness of adsorbents, and the DFP-derived biochar shows a fast adsorption rate for both NR and MV dyes, with equilibrium typically reached within 1 h ( Fig. 5a,b). This rapid adsorption rate is due to the porous structure and high surface area of biochar. The adsorption process can be divided into two distinct stages. The initial stage occurs within the first 20 min, characterized by a very rapid adsorption rate attributed to the interaction between surface binding sites and dye molecules. The second stage involves slower adsorption over approximately 40 min to reach equilibrium, likely due to the saturation of surface adsorption sites, which prompts adsorption sites on the inner surface of biochar to engage in dye uptake.

To further analyze the adsorption mechanism, correlation analyses were conducted using pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order, and intraparticle diffusion kinetic models (Fig. 5a-c and Fig. S3) (Din et al., 2017; Fan et al., 2019; Hameed, 2008). It is worth noting that the theoretical adsorption capacities (109.17 mg g−1 for NR and 90.09 mg g−1 for MV), calculated using the pseudo-second-order kinetic models ( Table 1), closely matched the experimentally measured values (101.2 mg g−1 for NR and 85.2 mg g−1 for MV). This indicates that the pseudo-second-order model is suitable for describing the adsorption process. Moreover, the linear fitting curves derived from the intraparticle diffusion model do not intersect with the origin, which implies that the intraparticle diffusion process does not solely dictate the rate-limiting step . Nonetheless, the plots reveal two distinct linear regions, indicating the joint influence of surface adsorption and intraparticle diffusion on the adsorption process.

Further investigations were carried out to examine the interaction between the DFP-based biochar and MV/NR dyes, with a specific emphasis on the impact of initial dye concentrations. The results revealed that the DFP-derived biochar displayed a high adsorption capacity when the dye concentrations were high (Fig. 5d,e). This can be attributed to the fact that an elevated dye concentration not only increased the chances of contact between the adsorption sites and dye molecules but also generated the necessary driving force for dye molecules to overcome the mass transfer resistance from the aqueous phase to the adsorbent phase. To gain a more comprehensive understanding of the adsorption performance, three well-known adsorption isotherm models including Langmuir, Freundlich, and Temkin isotherms (Chen et al., 2019; Chu et al., 2020; Wawrzkiewicz et al., 2022) were utilized to analyze the adsorption data (Fig. 5d-f and Fig. S4). A comparative analysis revealed that the Langmuir isotherm provided a superior fit to the adsorption data compared to the Freundlich and Temkin isotherms (Fig. 5f and Table 2). This suggests that the dye molecules adsorbed onto the biochar surface form a molecular monolayer, which implies no interaction between the adsorbed dye molecules, and multi-layer adsorption is excluded. Using the Langmuir isotherm, the maximum adsorption capacity (Qm) of the DFP-derived adsorbent for NR and MV was calculated to be 103.63 and 85.47 mg g−1, respectively. These values compare favorably to many reported biomass-derived adsorbents ( Table 3).

To gain a comprehensive understanding of the adsorption mechanism, a comparison was made between the FTIR spectra of DFT-derived biochar before and after the uptake of NR. In Fig. S5a, distinct absorption bands attributed to the vibrations of aromatic rings (e.g., 1590 and 724 cm−1) were clearly observed after NR adsorption, indicating the successful capture of NR molecules. Additionally, the absorption peaks associated with -OH groups shifted from 3420 to 3433 cm−1 after adsorption, suggesting that hydrogen bonding interactions also played a role in the adsorption process, alongside electrostatic interactions. Moreover, the regeneration capability of the DFT-derived biochar as an adsorbent was evaluated using ethanol as an eluting agent. As depicted in Fig. S5b, even after five successive cycles of adsorption-desorption experiments, the regenerated DFT-derived biochar demonstrated a relatively high removal efficiency (>75%) for both NR and MV dyes. Therefore, the DFP-derived biochar holds great potential for adsorptive removal of cationic dyes from water due to its cost-effective synthesis, fine adsorption selectivity, fast adsorption rate, high adsorption capacity, and favorable regeneration capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of DFP-derived CQDs for Fe3+ sensing and cell imaging

ce:section.ce:para: Following simple hydrothermal treatment of DFP, a liquid product containing highly luminescent CQDs was successfully produced. To examine the morphology of obtained CQDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observation was conducted. As shown in Fig. 6a, the CQDs exhibit a small and uniform size (3–6 nm), with no apparent aggregation. Raman spectrum (Fig. 6b) reveals the presence of distinct D and G bands at 1365 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1, respectively, suggesting the formation of carbon materials by hydrothermal treatment of DFP (Li et al., 2023; Preethi et al., 2021). The relative intensity (ID/IG) value of 0.76 suggested the inclusion of amorphous carbon within the CQDs. The FTIR spectrum indicated the presence of O-H, CC, CO, and C-O bonds (Fig. 6c). To further investigate the composition of CQDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 6d) confirmed that the CQDs mainly consist of C, O, and N elements, with other elements being negligible. The XPS C1s spectrum in Fig. 6e validated the presence of C-C, CC, C-O, and CO bonds, which correlated well with the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 6c). In addition, the XPS N1s spectrum (Fig. 6f) indicated the existence of pyridinic N (399.4 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.1 eV). The exceptional aqueous dispersibility and stability of the CQDs can be attributed to their small size and the existence of hydrophilic functional groups. Consequently, they can be effortlessly stored at room temperature for up to three months without perceptible precipitates.

The optical characteristics of CQDs were assessed using various methods including the UV–visible absorption, excitation, and emission spectra ( Fig. 7a). The aqueous solution of CQDs appears transparent under daylight but exhibits a bright blue fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm UV lamp. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of CQDs displays a distinct absorption peak at 277 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π * electronic transition associated with the CC bond of CQDs (Lu et al., 2018; Qiu et al., 2021). Similar to previously reported CQDs (Liao et al., 2016; Min et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2023), the emission wavelength of CQDs depends on the excitation wavelength (Fig. 7b). As the excitation wavelength increases from 315 nm to 425 nm, the corresponding emission peak shifts from 405 nm to 506 nm. The maximum emission peak occurs at 445 nm, which corresponds to the optimal excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CQDs was determined to be 4.6%, which is comparable to the values reported for numerous other CQDs made from biomass byproducts (Hsu et al., 2012; Sha et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014).

The CQDs also show favorable photostability, as demonstrated by the absence of any discernible decrease in fluorescence intensity even after 20 min of irradiation with 365 nm UV light (Fig. 7c). Furthermore, investigations were conducted to examine the effects of solution pH, electrolyte concentration, and storage duration on the fluorescent properties of CQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 7d-f, the CQDs exhibit remarkable fluorescence stability over a wide range of experimental conditions, including fluctuations in solution pH (in the range of 5–9) (Fig. 7d), elevated concentrations of NaCl electrolyte (Fig. 7e), and prolonged storage durations (e.g., up to 20 days) (Fig. 7f). These findings suggest that the CQDs possess exceptional fluorescence properties and hold significant potential for use as a fluorescent probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of Fe3+ by DFP-derived CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the DFP-derived CQDs exhibited a notable fluorescence response to Fe3+. Fig. 8a illustrates a gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs upon the addition of Fe3+. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (I/I0) displayed a favorable linear relationship (R2 = 0.9935) with Fe3+ concentration within the wide range of 0–285 μM (Fig. 8b). The limit of detection (LOD) of CQDs was determined to be 1.13 μM, with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which compares favorably to many reported biomass-derived CQDs ( Table 4). Meanwhile, the fluorescence quenching interaction between CQDs and Fe3+ was also found to be fast, taking only 2 min to complete (Fig. 8c). To gain insights into the sensing mechanism, the influence of Fe3+ on the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs was investigated. As depicted in Fig. 8d the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs was observed to decrease from 7.42 ns to 6.86 ns upon the addition of Fe3+, this signifies that the quenching of CQDs fluorescence by Fe3+ is most likely due to an electron transfer from CQDs to Fe3+. When CQDs are excited, the electrons transition from a ground state (S0) to an excited state (S1) (Fig. 8e). Subsequently, these electrons undergo non-radiative electron transfer by entering the half-full 3d orbital of Fe3+ resulting in the CQDs fluorescence quenching. Hence, the sensing mechanism of Fe3+ by CQDs is related to the electron transfer between the CQDs and Fe3+.

In practical sensing applications, it is crucial for fluorescent probes to exhibit high detection selectivity as well as sensitivity. Herein, we evaluated the selectivity of CQDs towards Fe3+ against a range of common metal ions, including Co2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Na+, and K+. The results depicted in Fig. 9a show that the blue fluorescence of CQDs was almost entirely quenched by 300 μM of Fe3+. In contrast, other metal ions caused only slight quenching effects (e.g. Co2+ and Mn2+) or no apparent quenching effects at all (e.g. Na+ and Ca2+). The relatively fluorescent intensity (I/I0) data presented in Fig. 9b further support the selective quenching of CQDs by Fe3+. Furthermore, we also investigated how the coexistence of other metal ions affects the fluorescence detection of Fe3+. The fluorescent spectra of CQDs solution when 150 μM of Fe3+ was introduced alone and when it was mixed with 150 μM of other metal ions were compared. As shown in Fig. 9c, the results indicate that other coexisting metal ions only had a slight quenching effect on the fluorescence of CQDs compared to Fe3+ alone. Based on the above results, we can conclude that the DFP-derived CQDs exhibit a wide linear detection range, low LOD, and excellent detection selectivity towards Fe3+, holding potential as an applicable fluorescent probe for Fe3+ sensing.

The practical applications of CQDs rely heavily on their low toxicity, therefore, we evaluated the toxicity of DFP-derived CQDs through thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) cell viability assays with HeLa and HepG2 cells (Malavika et al., 2021; Pal et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). After incubation with CQDs for 24 h, the cell viability remained close to 100% at low concentrations (≤50 μg/mL) and above 86% at high concentrations (e.g., 200 μg/mL) ( Fig. 10a,b), indicating their low toxicity. The CQDs were also studied for their potential as a fluorescent probe for cell imaging. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images of HeLa cells after incubation with CQDs at a concentration of 20 μg/mL revealed that all the cells maintained their normal morphology (Fig. 10c-f). The cellular cytoplasm emitted strong blue fluorescence, indicating that the CQDs were efficiently internalized and had favorable cell uptake capability (Fig. 10c,d). Furthermore, the CQDs showed potential for sensing Fe3+ within cells. After incubation with CQDs followed by the addition of 100 μM of Fe3+, the fluorescence in HeLa cells was significantly weaker than in the absence of Fe3+ (Fig. 10e,f). This suggests that the CQDs can be utilized as a fluorescent nanoprobe for detecting Fe3+ within cells.

In summary, we have demonstrated a simple and efficient method for producing cationic dyes (e.g., NR and MV) adsorbent and fluorescent CQDs simultaneously from DFP using a one-pot hydrothermal approach. The resulting adsorbent has a porous structure, high specific surface area and numerous oxygen-containing functional groups associated with the negatively charged surface. This adsorbent showed excellent adsorption capacity, rapid adsorption rate and high selectivity for cationic dyes. The adsorption process followed the pseudo-second-order kinetic and Langmuir isotherm models. The CQDs derived from DFP exhibited small and uniform size, superior water dispersibility, bright and tunable fluorescence, and unique fluorescence sensitivity to Fe3+. These CQDs were used successfully as a fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ with low LOD (1.13 μM) and a wide linear detection range (0–285 μM). The CQDs also showed low cytotoxicity and were readily taken up by cells for imaging and sensing of Fe3+ within cells. Given the cost-effective raw materials, straightforward synthesis and exceptional properties of the adsorbent and CQDs, this approach offers a promising strategy for value-added utilization of DFP and other biomass byproducts.
Zhou Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Tan Jisuan: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization.
